mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Bhutan on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the Himalayas. Bhutan is a small landlocked kingdom sandwiched between India and China (Tibet) 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Bhutan?
mapQuestions.answer: Thimphu, the only world capital with NO traffic lights (they tried a set once, took them out a week later because the locals preferred a hand-waving traffic officer).

mapQuestions.question: What language does Bhutan speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Dzongkha (the national language), plus Sharchopkha in the east, Nepali in the south, and around 20 other languages. English is the language of schools.

mapQuestions.question: How does Bhutan measure success?
mapQuestions.answer: Bhutan uses "Gross National Happiness" (GNH) instead of just GDP. It measures health, environment, time use, community and culture, all in deciding national policy. Invented by the Bhutanese king in 1972.

mapQuestions.question: How protected is Bhutan's environment?
mapQuestions.answer: Bhutan is the world's ONLY carbon-NEGATIVE country: its forests absorb more CO2 than the country emits. Over 70% of the country is forested by law.

foods.name: Ema datshi
foods.description: Bhutan's national dish: green or red chillies cooked in melted yak cheese. Not a side, not a sauce, the chillies are the MAIN ingredient. Spicy and creamy.

foods.name: Red rice
foods.description: A nutty pinkish-red rice grown only in the Himalayan terraces of Bhutan. Eaten with every meal.

foods.name: Momo
foods.description: Steamed Tibetan-style dumplings, filled with cheese, beef or vegetables, eaten with hot chilli sauce. The Bhutanese cold-day favourite.

foods.name: Phaksha paa
foods.description: Pork stewed slowly with radishes and dried red chillies. Hearty winter food in the highlands.

foods.name: Suja (butter tea)
foods.description: A salty, buttery yak-butter tea, churned with milk and salt, traditionally drunk at altitude to keep you warm and hydrated. Acquired taste.

foods.name: Khabzey
foods.description: Crispy fried twisted pastries eaten at festivals and celebrations, often slightly sweet, sometimes dipped in butter tea.

scavengerItems.label: A "dzong" (massive fortress-monastery, every Bhutanese town has one)
A snow-capped Himalayan peak (Bhutan's highest, Gangkhar Puensum, is the world's tallest unclimbed mountain)
Spinning prayer wheels along a monastery wall
Strings of colourful prayer flags fluttering on a mountain ridge
A man in a "gho" (the knee-length national robe, worn by most Bhutanese men)
A woman in a "kira" (the long woven national dress)
The Tiger's Nest Monastery (Paro Taktsang) hanging on a sheer cliff
A yak grazing in a high-mountain meadow
Buddhist monks (in red and yellow robes) chanting in a temple
An archery match (the Bhutanese national sport, played with traditional bows)
Friendly stray dogs everywhere (Bhutanese Buddhism teaches kindness to all living things)
A masked monk-dance ("cham") at a religious festival

geoMatches.description: The capital. Small (the only world capital with no traffic lights), surrounded by mountains, with the giant Buddha Dordenma statue on a hill overlooking it.
Home to Bhutan's only international airport (one of the world's most challenging to land at), and the famous Tiger's Nest Monastery on a cliff above the valley.
The old capital, with a stunning dzong sitting at the meeting point of two rivers. Visited for major royal ceremonies and weddings.
A high central valley region with several of the oldest Buddhist temples in Bhutan and a quieter, more rural feel.
Paro Taktsang Monastery, built into a sheer cliff face 900 m above the Paro valley. Reached by a 2 to 3 hour uphill hike. One of the most photographed places in Asia.
At 7,570 m, the highest mountain in Bhutan and the highest UNCLIMBED mountain in the world. Bhutan has banned mountaineering on its sacred peaks since 1994.

senses.smell: Juniper incense smoke at temples, melting yak butter, fresh mountain air, woodsmoke from a village fire, the warm tang of melting cheese with chillies…
senses.hear: Prayer wheels turning, monks chanting, "Kuzu zangpo!" greetings, archery shouts in a village field, the wind through Himalayan prayer flags, mountain rivers below the road…
senses.taste: Fiery cheese-and-chilli ema datshi, nutty red rice, hot steamed momos, salty butter tea, sweet honeyed pastries at a festival…
senses.feel: Thin Himalayan air, soft wool of a traditional gho or kira, the cold stone of a dzong wall, the smooth carved wood of a prayer wheel handle, the warm flank of a yak…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What is Gross National Happiness?
stories.body: In 1972, the young Bhutanese king Jigme Singye Wangchuck announced something unusual: instead of measuring his country's success only by GDP (Gross Domestic Product, basically how much money the country produces), Bhutan would also measure "Gross National Happiness" (GNH). GNH covers psychological well-being, physical health, education, cultural diversity, time use, community, environment and good governance. Bhutanese policy is shaped by these things, not just by economic numbers. If a new road would damage a sacred forest, GNH might decide against the road. If a policy would damage community bonds, GNH might suggest a different policy. The idea has spread, and the UN now has its own International Day of Happiness (20 March) inspired by Bhutan.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your country measured success by happiness as much as by money, what would change?

stories.question: How do you build a monastery on a cliff?
stories.body: The Tiger's Nest Monastery (Paro Taktsang) clings to a sheer cliff face 900 m above the Paro valley. According to Bhutanese legend, the Buddhist master Guru Rinpoche flew to this exact spot on the back of a tigress in the 8th century, meditated in a cave there for 3 years, 3 months, 3 days and 3 hours, and so the spot became one of the most sacred in the Himalayas. The current monastery was built around the cave in 1692. A massive fire in 1998 destroyed most of it; it was painstakingly rebuilt by hand using the same materials and techniques. Getting to it now still requires a 2 to 3 hour uphill walk. Hardly anyone who climbs to Tiger's Nest comes back unaffected.

stories.question: Why is Bhutan carbon-NEGATIVE?
stories.body: Most countries' factories, cars and power stations release more CO2 (the gas that warms the planet) than their forests can absorb. Bhutan is the OPPOSITE. Bhutan's constitution requires that at least 60% of the country must always stay forested (it is actually around 71% today). Those huge intact forests soak up about 3 times more CO2 than the country emits. That makes Bhutan the only carbon-negative country on Earth (Suriname is the only other one sometimes counted as similar). The Bhutanese argue that this is one of their proudest contributions to the world: cleaning up CO2 that other, bigger, richer countries have produced.

stories.question: Why does everyone wear a gho or kira?
stories.body: Bhutan has a national dress code: men wear the "gho" (a knee-length robe tied with a belt) and women wear the "kira" (a long woven dress with a jacket). Schoolchildren, civil servants and people visiting government offices or temples are required to wear them. The dress code was made official in 1989 to protect Bhutanese cultural identity in a globalising world. Far from feeling forced, most Bhutanese are proud of the gho and kira. The fabrics are often hand-woven and tell stories through their patterns. It is one of the most visible ways Bhutan keeps its old culture alive.

stories.question: Why are Bhutan's mountains unclimbed?
stories.body: In 1994, the Bhutanese government decided that climbing any mountain higher than 6,000 m would be BANNED, to respect the religious belief that those peaks are home to gods and spirits. As a result, Bhutan has the highest unclimbed mountain on Earth: Gangkhar Puensum, 7,570 m, never summited and not allowed to be. While Everest, K2 and the other big Himalayan peaks have all been climbed (sometimes hundreds of times), Bhutan's biggest mountains remain entirely untouched by humans. From Bhutanese viewpoints you can stare at peaks that have NEVER been climbed and never will be.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Bhutan so far?
Have you tried saying "kuzu zangpo" or "kadrinche" yet?
Tiger's Nest, dzong, valley, monastery, which has felt the most "Bhutan"?
Gross National Happiness instead of GDP, what does that change?
Did you walk to Tiger's Nest? How was the climb?
Bhutan has banned mountaineering on its sacred peaks. How does that feel as a way to respect a place?

wordSearch.hint: Bhutanese foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Yak
animals.description: Long-haired highland cattle, kept by Bhutanese nomadic families for milk, cheese and wool. Above 3,000 m.

animals.name: Takin
animals.description: Bhutan's national animal. Looks like a cross between a goat and a moose, with a curving nose. Found in alpine meadows.

animals.name: Black-necked crane
animals.description: A rare crane that winters in Bhutan's Phobjikha Valley (October to February). The Bhutanese people consider them sacred.

animals.name: Himalayan vulture
animals.description: A massive scavenger with a 3 m wingspan, gliding above the high passes.

animals.name: Bhutanese street dog
animals.description: Friendly, well-fed dogs everywhere in Bhutanese towns. Buddhism teaches kindness to all living things.

animals.name: Red panda
animals.description: A small fluffy rust-coloured tree-climber in Bhutan's east. Wild population is very small.

animals.name: Bengal tiger
animals.description: Bhutan has the highest-altitude tigers on Earth, found at over 4,000 m. Extremely rare to see.
